text: It's not often we get to have really positive news stories like this one around cancer.
HPV vaccination works and saves lives.
Statistics are now proving the facts.
I've got girls, my eldest has had hers and my second one will be having it.
So it's really important.
I would definitely encourage everyone to accept that.
Here's a vaccine that can prevent cancer in the future.
Why wouldn't you have it?
is a medical breakthrough, a vaccine that is gradually eliminating a deadly cancer.
The number of young women dying from cervical cancer has plummeted in England following the rollout of the HPV vaccine.
We're going to tell you why it is making such an impact and whether this type of vaccine could revolutionise cancer care.
So the basics.
HPV stands for human papillomavirus, a common group of viruses which can lead to serious health problems.
There is a vaccine for this and now landmark research shows
that cervical cancer deaths have fallen sharply since that vaccine started being given to school -aged girls.
Here are some of the key findings of that report.
For the first time on record between 2020 and 2024, no women in their early 20s died of cervical cancer.
And women below the age of 30 had an almost zero risk of death.
Experts say around 200 lives have been saved as a direct result of the vaccine.
Cath Burns is our health expert.
So Cath, let's start with some of the fundamental questions.
First of all, what is HPV?
Well, we talk about it as a virus.
It's actually a group of about 100 viruses.
They're very common.
They are very contagious.
Most of us will have had them at some point and probably not even noticed because it often goes away without
causing any issues but sometimes it can cause abnormal cell changes that years later could go on to cause cancer like cervical cancer.
In terms of how you catch it, it's really easy to catch.
You can get it from sex but it doesn't just have to be sex it can be any kind of close skin -to -skin contact with your genitals or from sharing things like sex toys.
Condoms do help but because they don't entirely cover that area they don't entirely protect against it.
And can you tell us a little bit more about the vaccine who exactly can get it how does it work?
So schoolgirls in the UK have been offered it since 2008 essentially it stops cancer
before it starts boys have been offered it since 2019 and it helps protect them against anal, penis,
throat and mouth cancer as well as stopping them from passing on the HPV virus it's
offered essentially to all school schoolchildren, secondary schoolchildren aged 11 to 13 in the UK.
And if you've missed your vaccine, you can go and get it from your GP up to the age of 25.
Well, Kath, stay with us.
Let's turn now to one of the consequences of the HPV virus, cervical cancer, which is the fourth most common cancer in women globally.
Well, on the right, you can see Alexandra Legge.
She left school just before the HPV vaccine was introduced in England.
In 2021, she was diagnosed with cervical cancer at age 30.
She underwent intensive treatment, although surgeons were able to preserve a small part of her cervix.
Just a year later, her daughter Ivy was born.
Alexandra is now an advocate for the vaccine.
I just remember hearing the words and I just everything went through my head.
It was just yeah, it was so hard, but at the same time I knew something wasn't right.
So it was So, such a shock, but knowing something wasn't right, it just answered all of my questions and fears that I had.
I'm a real advocate for this vaccine because statistics are now proving the facts.
Smear tests now specifically look for HPV to reduce the risk.
And I know when Ivy is old enough, I will be taking her.
She will be first in the queue.
I'm not letting her not have this.
So Cath, can you tell us a bit more about cervical cancer?
What are the symptoms and how is it treated?
Well, let's start with the absolute basics.
The cervix is the opening between the vagina and the womb.
This kind of cancer is most common in women aged 30 to 35, but it can happen at any time.
About 2 ,000 women every day get diagnosed with it worldwide.
In the UK, the government has pledged to eliminate it by 2040.
With symptoms, the important thing to say is we're talking about symptoms that are not usual for you so that could be vaginal bleeding that isn't usual for you
maybe during or after sex maybe between periods or during the menopause also changes in discharge pain during sex
pain in your lower back and in your tummy it's really important to say every woman out here will go they are symptoms of lots of other conditions
so it's about what's usual for you if you're worried though do go to your gp because early diagnosis leads to better treatment options.
OK, well, the number of people dying from cervical cancer is expected to keep falling, and that is as more people get the HPV jab and vaccinated people grow older.
Cancer Research UK funded the latest study and their chief executive celebrated the scale of this achievement.
The new research demonstrates that HPV vaccination works and saves lives,
reduces the number of deaths from cervical cancer, and we have the possibility of eliminating cervical cancer as a public health issue here in the UK.
Well, she spoke about eliminating cervical cancer as a public health issue entirely.
Kath, that sounds really optimistic.
How much of an impact could a successful vaccine campaign actually now have?
That's the thing.
As a health correspondent, it is so rare to hear experts or scientists talk about elimination.
You want that kind of conversation, you just don't get it.
and they're also calling it an incredible milestone.
So they're saying that these figures are actually going to be the tip of the iceberg because as more generations get vaccinated and protected, more lives are going to be saved.
I think really though, the best example for us to look at is Australia because they were the first country worldwide
to come up with this vaccine and they are not seeing any new cases in women under 25.
Their aim is to eliminate cervical cancer by 2035 and they think they're going to do it early.
One of the main stumbling blocks potentially to eliminating cervical cancer is the uptake of the HPV vaccine,
while charities are warning that too few teenagers and young people are coming forward to get the jab.
So we were around 90 % in terms of vaccination rates in England and across the UK.
It's now around 75%.
Around one in four children are leaving school without the protection from the HPV vaccine.
Covid meant the vaccination programme stalled slightly, but there is also some people who are hesitant around vaccines.
So we would say that we want to try and break down those barriers.
We want to provide accurate information and let people know that this vaccine has been used for many, many years now across the world and it is safe and extremely effective.
So, Kath, what are some of the main challenges to vaccine uptake?
I think, Geeta, it's really worth going over those figures again.
They want it to be 90%.
It's actually 75%.
And she spoke then about the COVID pandemic.
Vaccine hesitancy has been an issue since the pandemic, but also before that.
And we can see the real world impacts of that now.
We're seeing measles cases go up as we know that MMR vaccination rates are nowhere near their need to be.
When we talk about vaccine hesitancy, there are some people who are just anti -vax.
In reality, though, it's a lot of people who are just hesitant.
They don't feel they've got enough good information being given to them.
They're dealing with a lot of misinformation.
Sometimes it's just the reality of juggling school and work and these appointments.
With this particular vaccine, though, experts are talking about another kind of issue, that some parents are worried that this might encourage their children to become sexually active,
to have unprotected sex.
The public health answer to that is that the earlier you get protection here, the longer lasting immunity you're more likely to build up.
So it's important to get it before you become sexually active.
And the HPV vaccine is already saving hundreds of lives.
As we've been saying, scientists believe it could eliminate the cancer altogether in the near future.
That success is something that many other areas of cancer research would like to replicate.
So Kath, what can we learn overall from the success of this vaccine and what is the big picture for cancer research and treatment globally?
Well cancer researchers will be looking at this vaccine and thinking right what are they doing and we can copy it.
So some of the clinical trials I can tell you about are also looking at preventative vaccines so they're trying to stop cancer before it starts.
One of them is for lung cancer.
Now this one is targeting people who are at high risk of developing lung cancer.
It essentially uses the same technology as COVID vaccines, and it trains the immune system to recognise certain proteins that are linked to the disease.
There's another one with ovarian cancer, and again, this is targeted at women who are at high risk of getting it,
and it's going to prime the immune system to recognise certain abnormal cell changes before they turn into cancer.
So there is a lot of work here and a lot of hope too.
Kath Burns, thank you.
And that is some of the key points that you need to know about the vaccine eliminating a deadly cancer.
